details.text: Derivative: الْقَارِعَةُ is an اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il) which is known in English as the active participle which means it’s a noun that does something like the Word: Teacher, speaker and farmer. Here الْقَارِعَةُ (Al-Qaari’ah) is describing something that is engaged in the act of doing something. الْقَارِعَةُ comes from قّرَعَ - قَرْع which refers to when something strikes something hard and produces an intense, loud and disturbing sound or noise. It is described as a striking that is strong and has intent and resolve behind it. It is used to describe someone rapping or knocking hard on the door at night as it disturbs you, shocks you and takes you by surprise. Figuratively it can also refer to a tough or trying situation because it disturbs you and takes away your peace. الْقَارِعَةُ is one of the words used in the Qur’an to describe the Day of Judgement. It is from the words which Allah has given a specific meaning to which was unknown or not applied prior to the revelation. Its linguistic meaning was understood but its application to this reality was unknown. It has been said that it refers to the striking of the hearts of the people and creating a disturbance within them due to the terror associated with the horrors of that day. It can also refer to the striking and crashing that will occur as a result of the calamitous events of that day.

الْقَارِعَةُ

- الْقَارِعَةُ (Al-Qaari’ah) is a single word and speech in Arabic is normally composed of two or more words. This has led some to state that there is a word that is omitted (known as حَذْف – Hadhf) but can be deduced. The missing word could be a خَبر (Khabar predicate) which follows Al-Qaari’ah or a فِعْل (F’il – Verb) which precedes Al-Qaari’ah, which carry the meaning of “to be coming”. Another possible explanation is that the word stands alone and is being used like a warning like the word “Fire” to indicate to a state of emergency or urgency and does not require further elaboration.
Derivative: هِيَهْ – the extra ه (Haa’) added to هِيَ (Hiya – It) is for the purpose of magnifying and to give extra weight to the word to add to the terror associated with it.

وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا هِيَهْ

- When وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا “What will make you know what” has been used in the Qur’an it is followed by a full noun however here it is uses a pronoun “هِيَهْ” (it). This shortened reference to the thing by using its pronoun can indicate to the brevity of the elaboration that is about to follow.
Derivative: حَامِيَةٌ is from حَمِيَ and is describing the extreme heat, burning and blazing attributes of the fire of hell.

- مَا أَدْرَاكَ in the past tense means: “What would make you know” whilst مَا يُدْرِيكَ in the present tense means: “What will make you know”. When مَا أَدْرَاكَ is used in the Qur’an Allah provides the answer but when مَا يُدْرِيكَ is used Allah does not provide the answer and leave it open ended.
Derivative: مَبْثُوثِ from بَثَّ and means to be spread out wide and dispersed i.e., separated from each other.

Derivative: الْفَرَاشِ is the plural of فَرَاشِة refers to moths and insects that fly around light. The use of الْفَرَاشِ reflects chaotic movement just like moths fly in a haphazard direction and do not fly together in an ordered fashion.
Derivative: الْعِهْنِ refers to wool of different textures and colours.

Derivative: الْمَنْفُوشِ refers to the process of carding wool and in the context of الْقَارِعَةُ which means “striking” it can refer to the striking and beating of the wool making it ruffled, puffed up and not tightly intertwined. It implies lightness and weakness.

Derivative: مَوَازِينُ can be the plural of two Word: مِيزان (Meezaan) which means “a scale” which is used to weigh and مَوْزُون (Mawzoon) which means “weighed” and refers to the object or item that is being weighed on a scale. مَوَازِينُ can therefore refer to those things which are being weighed and not the scales themselves.

فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ

- “Then as for the one (فَأَمَّا مَن) whose scales are heavy (ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ)”. This has been interpreted as meaning whoever’s Hasanaat (good deeds) outweigh his Sayyi’aat (bad deeds) in the case where the scale is perceived to be one with two sides, one with good deeds and the other with bad. Another understanding is that it is only talking about the good deeds as bad needs are understood to not carry any weight.
Derivative: عِيشَة comes from عَيْش(‘Aish) refers to a life in which the necessities of life are not a source of concern or a life without worries.

Derivative: رَاضِيَة means “pleasing” like مُرْضِيَة (Murdiyah) or in a state of pleasure, contentment and satisfaction. It is in the اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) form because the life it is describing (عِيشَةٍ) provides pleasure and contentment (الرِّضاء – Ar-Ridaa’) in and from itself.
Derivative: خَفَّتْ means to be light in weight.

Derivative: هَاوِيَةٌ comes from هَوَى – يَهْوِي – هُوِي which refers falling into a deep canyon. It is used when a bird of prey swoops far down from up high to its prey which is faster than falling. هَاوِيَةٌ is a chasm that is extremely deep, a bottomless pit or abyss and refers to the depths of hellfire.

Derivative: أُمُّ literally means mother. It is used here to mean his destination and home. It is being referred to as a mother because a child runs to its mother, a mother wraps herself around a child and clings to him and also because the child’s home is in the womb of his mother. It implies a place that will cling to him and not let him escape.
